cuisine: Western / Greek — widely retailed in Singapore (NTUC FairPrice, Cold Storage, Redmart)
region_of_origin: Greece (straining tradition); most Singapore retail product produced in Australia or New Zealand
food_subgroup: Strained full-fat yoghurt — produced by straining regular yoghurt through cloth or membrane filters to remove whey, yielding a thicker, higher-protein, lower-lactose product

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for strained full-fat Greek yoghurt; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

main_ingredients: live bacterial cultures (Lactobacillus bulgaricus, Streptococcus thermophilus)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard single-serve container of full-fat Greek yoghurt weighing 150g, as commonly sold at NTUC FairPrice and Cold Storage in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Yogurt, Greek, plain, whole milk — USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 171304
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, SR Legacy Foods Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy FDC 171304 is a direct match for plain whole-milk Greek yoghurt. All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are sourced from this entry. Values are consistent with the input record and have been retained as verified.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Plain Full-Fat Yoghurt — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 11 (range 10–15) for plain full-fat yoghurt sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database. Greek (strained) yoghurt has a comparable or slightly lower GI than regular yoghurt due to higher protein and lower lactose content from the straining process. GL per 150g serving = (11 × 3.6 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.59, rounded to 0.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (36mg/100g; ~54mg per 150g serving) with beneficial potassium content (141mg/100g; ~212mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Full-fat Greek yoghurt is suitable for hypertensive individuals; its very low sodium content and favourable Na:K ratio support blood pressure management, though the moderate saturated fat (~5g per 150g serving) should be considered in the context of overall dietary fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely low glycemic index (GI ~11) and glycemic load (~0.6 per 150g serving) with high casein protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Full-fat Greek yoghurt is an excellent snack choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the combination of GI ~11, high casein protein (9g/100g), moderate fat, and lactic acid produces minimal postprandial glucose excursion, making it one of the most blood glucose-neutral dairy foods available.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (3.3g/100g; ~5g per 150g serving) offset by high protein, low sodium, and beneficial micronutrient profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Full-fat Greek yoghurt can be included in a heart-healthy diet in moderate portions; while it contains ~5g saturated fat per 150g serving, its low sodium, high potassium, and high-quality protein profile are broadly cardioprotective, and current evidence does not support restricting full-fat fermented dairy in cardiovascular disease prevention.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High calcium (~165mg per 150g serving) and complete casein protein (PDCAAS 1.0) supporting bone and muscle health in RA
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Full-fat Greek yoghurt is beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its high calcium content supporting bone density, complete protein supporting muscle preservation, and Vitamin B12 (~1.1mcg per 150g) supporting neurological health; monitor saturated fat intake in RA patients with elevated cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Full-fat Greek yoghurt is a strained fermented dairy product made by removing whey from regular whole-milk yoghurt through cloth or membrane filtration, resulting in a thick, creamy, protein-dense food with a characteristic tangy flavour and smooth texture. Per 100g it provides 97 kcal, 9g protein (predominantly slow-digesting casein), 5g fat (3.3g saturated), and only 3.6g carbohydrate (low lactose due to straining). The glycemic index is extremely low at approximately 11 (University of Sydney GI Database), with a glycemic load of only 0.6 per standard 150g serving, making it one of the most blood glucose-neutral dairy foods available. It is an excellent source of calcium (110mg/100g), phosphorus (135mg/100g), riboflavin (0.18mg/100g), and Vitamin B12 (0.75mcg/100g). In Singapore, full-fat Greek yoghurt is widely available at NTUC FairPrice, Cold Storage, and Redmart under brands including Chobani Full Fat (Australian-produced), Farmers Union Greek Style, and Pauls Organic Greek. It is suitable for vegetarians, halal consumers, and those with gluten intolerance. Clinically, it is recommended as a post-exercise recovery food (casein protein), a diabetic-friendly snack (GI ~11), and a dairy introduction option for lactose-sensitive individuals. The straining process concentrates protein to approximately twice that of regular yoghurt and reduces lactose content significantly. Nutrient data sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC 171304.
common_user_queries: is greek yoghurt good for diabetes
greek yoghurt vs regular yoghurt protein
meal_context: Singapore supermarket NTUC FairPrice Cold Storage

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC 171304, an approved primary source with an exact match to plain whole-milk Greek yoghurt. GI value sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are fully populated from verified data. No estimation or analogy was required for primary nutritional values.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g set to -1: the MUFA breakdown is not separately reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC 171304 for this specific entry; only total fat and saturated fat are confirmed at this level of detail. polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: the PUFA breakdown is not separately reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC 171304 for this specific entry; USDA FDC 171304 does not disaggregate total fat into MUFA and PUFA components for this record. water_g set to -1: moisture content is not separately extracted or reported in the USDA SR Legacy FDC 171304 entry consulted for this record. trans_fat_g is confirmed 0.0 per USDA FDC 171304 (naturally negligible in fermented dairy). dietary_fibre_g is confirmed 0.0 — naturally zero as yoghurt is a dairy product containing no dietary fibre. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as dairy products contain negligible vitamin C. vitamin_e_mg is 0.0 — confirmed trace/negligible per USDA FDC 171304. The 'Greek' designation refers to the straining method, not necessarily Greek geographic origin; most Singapore retail product is produced in Australia (Chobani AU, Farmers Union AU). GI of 11 applies to plain full-fat yoghurt; flavoured or sweetened variants will have substantially higher GI and GL values. Serving size of 150g reflects standard single-serve retail container size in Singapore.
